request.question: In which year was the album released which included the single "Long Kiss Goodnight", which was used as a sample in the song "Rap name"?

title: Sons of the P
Rap Name
Disneyland Forever
Yesterday (Tyler Shaw album)
Tyler Shaw
Let Me Rock You
Charli Baltimore
Michael Licari
Life After Death
Kiss Goodnight
sentences: Sons of the P is the second album by American rap group Digital Underground, released on October 15, 1991.

The album featured two hit singles, "No Nose Job" and "Kiss You Back", both of which were written by, and featured the lead vocals of Greg Jacobs (a.k.a. Shock G/Humpty Hump), and the latter of which featured multi-layered choruses and background vocals sung by Boni Boyer, who briefly worked with D.U. shortly after her stint with Prince's Sign of the Times/Love Sexy band.

It is sometimes mistakenly reported that Kiss You Back was co-written & co-performed by George Clinton, but his name appropriately appears in the writers credit due to a sample of "(Not Just) Knee Deep" by Funkadelic.

He did however actively participate in the writing and recording of the title track "Sons of the P", to which he also contributed vocals, and which marked one of the earliest studio guest appearances by Clinton on a Hip Hop release, preceded only by Kurtis Blow's "Magilla Gorilla" released in 1986.
Both the album and the single "Kiss You Back" were each certified Gold by the RIAA.
"Rap Name" is the debut single of American rapper Obie Trice, released as a limited edition vinyl recording, taken from the deluxe edition of the soundtrack to the film "8 Mile".
The song features vocals from fellow-label mate Eminem, who sampled the track in two tracks for his album "The Eminem Show", released earlier in 2002.
These tracks are "Drips" and the intro of the album's lead single, "Without Me", as well as a few other lines from the song.
The song also features a vocal sample of The Notorious B.I.G. from his song "Long Kiss Goodnight" from the album "Life After Death".
The official video was filmed in Detroit with featured cameo appearances by Eminem's group D12.
Disneyland Forever was a nighttime spectacular at Disneyland that premiered alongside the "Paint the Night" parade and "World of Color—Celebrate!
" on May 21, 2015 as part of the park's 60th anniversary celebration.
The show was produced by Walt Disney Creative Entertainment, under direction of Steve Davison.
"Disneyland Forever" incorporates fireworks, projection mapping, fire, lasers, and searchlights to depict scenes from Disney films.
The show closed on September 5th, 2016 as the Diamond Celebration concluded along with World of Color Celebrate.
The shows exit song "A Kiss Goodnight" would be later be released on CD with a book bearing the songs name.
Yesterday is the debut studio album recorded by Canadian singer-songwriter Tyler Shaw, released September 4, 2015 through Tyler Ip, a label imprint of Sony Music Canada.
It was preceded by the release of four singles between 2012 and 2015, including the Gold-certified lead single, "Kiss Goodnight".
Shaw co-wrote twelve of the thirteen songs, including all four of the aforementioned singles.
Tyler Shaw (born April 8, 1993) is a Canadian singer and the winner of the "MuchMusic Coca-Cola Covers Contest 2012" organized by the specialized Canadian music cable channel Much (TV channel).
He rose to fame through the contest and subsequently released his debut single, "Kiss Goodnight", through Sony Music Canada in December 2012.
The song was certified Gold by Music Canada in April 2013.
Shaw's debut studio album, "Yesterday", was released on September 4, 2015.
Let Me Rock You is the third album released by former Kiss drummer Peter Criss.
Due to poor sales for his previous album, "Out of Control", "Let Me Rock You" was not released in the United States until 1998, when it was reissued on CD.
The album was produced by Vini Poncia, who previously produced Criss's 1978 solo album (officially released as a Kiss album).
"Let Me Rock You" features the song "Feels Like Heaven", written by Criss's former bandmate, Gene Simmons.
Vinnie Cusano also co-wrote a song for the album; at the same year the album was released, Cusano became known as Vinnie Vincent when he replaced Kiss guitarist Ace Frehley.
The album cover features Peter Criss for the first time without his Kiss makeup, as he didn't appear on the cover of his solo album "Out of Control".
One year later, Kiss also decided to take off their makeup for their "Lick It Up" album.
Tiffany Lane (born August 16, 1974), better known as Charli Baltimore, is an American rapper–songwriter.
Her stage name is taken from Geena Davis's character in the film "The Long Kiss Goodnight".
Michael Licari is an American television composer and songwriter.
He has worked as a composer on the television series, "Deliver Me", documentary "Triple the Triplets" (2005).

He has also worked as a song writer and performer on the 2007 film "Charlie Bartlett" (for the song "Nice Vibes"), and on the 1996 film "The Long Kiss Goodnight", as an arranger (for the "Jingle Bells Marching Band").
He has also worked on award-winning drama series, "The Guiding Light", "Another World","As The World Turns", "Sunset Beach", and "Santa Barbara".
Life After Death is the second and final studio album by American rapper The Notorious B.I.G., released on March 25, 1997, on Bad Boy Records and Arista Records.
A double album, it was released posthumously following his death on March 9, 1997.
It features collaborations with guest artists such as 112, Jay-Z, Lil' Kim, Mase, Bone Thugs-n-Harmony, Too $hort, Angela Winbush, D.M.C. of Run-D.M.C.
, R. Kelly, The LOX and Puff Daddy.
"Life After Death" exhibits The Notorious B.I.G. further delving into the mafioso rap subgenre.
The album is a sequel to his first album, "Ready to Die", and picks up where the last song, "Suicidal Thoughts", ends.
"Kiss Goodnight" is the debut single released by Canadian singer-songwriter Tyler Shaw.
The song was written by Shaw in conjunction with Stephen Kozmeniuk and Todd Clark, and was produced by Kozmeniuk and Zubin Thakkar.

It was released December 4, 2012 through Sony Music Canada as Shaw's first official single following his win of the second CocaCola MuchMusic Covers Contest (2012) and serves as the lead single for Shaw's debut studio album, "Yesterday" (2015).
A franglais version featuring vocals by french pop artist Nathalie Noël, entitled "Je veux juste rester là" ("I Just Want to Stay There"), was released April 9, 2013.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Role and goal: Summarize the first-hop evidence so the next agent can find the missing second-hop information. Produce a single short, factual `summary` that: (1) compresses the grounded facts in the provided passages; (2) exposes the bridge entity, the specific missing fact, or the retrieval direction needed for the next hop; and (3) gives concrete evidence anchors (quoted or clearly paraphrased phrases) that a retriever can use.

Content rules:
- Keep the summary concise and fact-focused. Do not attempt to produce the final answer; describe only what the passages support and what is still needed.
- Preserve the same passage facts and phrasing as you would otherwise (you may quote or paraphrase). Do not change which facts are exposed; only condense them.
- You may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts from the passages to state a bridge or to justify a next-hop direction. Do not invent facts, infer new temporal facts, or treat composition as unsupported when the necessary premises are present.
- If the passages lack a required premise, or contain conflicting identity links, explicitly state which premise or identity link is missing or ambiguous so the next agent can retrieve or disambiguate it.

Date-format rule (narrow formatting change):
- When the summary includes a date token that appears in the passages (any calendar date or parenthetical date taken from the text), render that date using the canonical form FullMonthName day, year (for example, "September 27, 2017") inside the evidence description. This normalization only changes the string form of the date token; it must not add, infer, or imply any additional temporal facts, nor be used as a reason to produce a final answer.
- Otherwise, leave all other wording and evidence presentation unchanged. Preserve quotations or clear paraphrases of the same passage text; only adjust the date token's string format.

Output contract:
- Return exactly one field `summary` containing the compressed first-hop evidence, the bridge/missing-fact signal, and any retrieval direction needed for the next hop. Do not output final answers, extra metadata, or new fields.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Produce exactly one compact retrieval query string (single line) in the `query` field and nothing else. The query should be a concise keyword-style search targeted to recover the missing bridge or supporting evidence needed by downstream agents.

General behavior:
- Use the visible facts in summary_1 as anchors (album titles, artist names, dates, relation terms) and compose a focused query that is likely to retrieve passages supporting the needed relation. Prefer concrete anchors and relation keywords over full sentences.
- If summary_1 already includes explicit profession/role/descriptor tokens for an anchor (for example, 'singer', 'director', 'country music artist'), omit those redundant descriptor tokens and emit a compact anchor-only query consisting of the core visible anchors (names, titles, groups). Retain relation tokens only when the question explicitly requests them or when disambiguation rules demand them. Do not remove descriptors when the question explicitly asks for role/occupation evidence or when a short-title disambiguation rule requires adding an entertainment disambiguator.
- Do not include explanatory text, user-facing prompts, or requests-for-clarification in the `query` field. Do not invent or assert missing facts (for example, do not supply an artist name that is not present in summary_1).
- Preserve the single-query output contract: emit exactly one string and no other fields or commentary.

Disambiguation for short/ambiguous title tokens:
- If an anchor in summary_1 is a short or potentially ambiguous title token, do not automatically append an entertainment-domain keyword. Only append a plain entertainment disambiguator keyword (for example: film movie album song novel author director) when the original question itself contains explicit entertainment-role words (e.g., film, movie, album, song, directed, author, novella) or when the question explicitly lists multiple title-like tokens. In those cases, include the visible anchor(s) from summary_1 plus the appropriate entertainment keyword to bias retrieval. If the question lacks entertainment cues and does not list multiple titles, do not guess or add an entertainment disambiguator—keep the query anchored to the visible tokens only.

Special-case for missing album artist or genre (narrow semantic boundary):
- If summary_1 shows an album title but does not include the album's artist or genre, or if summary_1 explicitly signals inability to determine genre or explicitly asks for the artist/passage (phrases such as "can't determine" or "please provide the artist"), then emit exactly one compact search phrase formatted as:
  <AlbumTitle> album artist genre
  Use only the visible album title as it appears in summary_1 followed by the three keywords: album artist genre. Do not add punctuation, explanatory words, alternate tokens, or candidate artist names.
- Do not apply this special-case if summary_1 already contains the artist or genre, or if the album anchor is an ambiguous short token that requires human clarification.

Fallback and downstream behavior:
- If retrieval using the emitted query returns no useful passages, the downstream agent may then ask the user for clarification or additional context. Do not ask the user inside the `query` field.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Task: Integrate the first-hop `context` with the newly retrieved `passages` and produce a single concise `summary` that preserves the facts needed by the final answer. Resolve the earlier bridge when the supplied passages support doing so; retain uncertainty only when the post-retrieval evidence is genuinely insufficient, ambiguous, or conflicting.

Guidelines:
- Treat a concise answer in `context` as the primary claim. Do not replace that concise context answer unless the `passages` contain a direct, identity-aligned contradiction.
- Before attributing any fact from a passage to the entity or relation named in `context`, confirm identity alignment (clear aliasing or explicit linking). Do not assume two similarly named mentions are the same without such alignment.
- When the context contains a concise bridge claim about a named entity (or otherwise names candidate/bridge entities), treat the context claim as the summary anchor and perform explicit identity-alignment checks before using passage facts. For each entity explicitly named in `context` as a candidate or bridge, do the following: if at least one passage provides an explicit, identity-aligned statement linking that named entity (or a clearly indicated alias) to the asserted relation, append a single brief corroborating sentence that paraphrases that linking fact and references the corroborating passage and also include one short clause for that context-named entity giving the passage-supported relation value (e.g., "Name: [relation value]."); if no passage provides the relation value for that context-named entity, append a short clause stating that no relation value is provided in the evidence (e.g., "Name: no [relation] provided in evidence."). Do not create such clauses for entities that are only mentioned in `passages` and are not named in `context`. Do not append corroboration if the `context` already contains the identical corroborating fact verbatim. Keep these additions short and keep the overall summary compact.
- If the `passages` are weak, ambiguous, or conflict with each other or with the context claim (but do not provide an explicit, identity-aligned contradiction), preserve the context claim and append a single short clause that flags the uncertainty or contradiction. When flagging, you may optionally include a verbatim quoted phrase from a passage of up to 10 words to show the source wording. Do not invent resolutions beyond what the `passages` show.
- If the `passages` contain an explicit, identity-aligned contradiction to the context claim, state that the passages contradict the context claim and briefly note the conflicting passage fact (optionally with a ≤10-word quote). Only in this case may the summary stop asserting the context claim as definitive.
- Keep the `summary` compact (aim for 1–3 sentences). Use uncertainty language only when a required premise, identity link, or relation is genuinely missing, ambiguous, or conflicting. Do not default to uncertainty when composition across context and passages licenses a conclusion.
- You may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts from `context` and `passages` to form a licensed multi-hop conclusion; the supporting facts need not all appear in a single passage.
- Do not include long verbatim quotes, do not invent facts, and do not attempt to resolve contradictions beyond reporting them as present in the `passages`.

Output: a single `summary` string that follows the rules above.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the field `answer`.

Use only the information in the provided summaries as evidence. summary_2 is the later, integrated evidence state and should be the primary source for deciding the minimal answer; summary_1 may contain useful first-hop compression or retrieval-oriented language and may be used to supply premises that, together with summary_2, license a conclusion. Do not invent facts or use outside knowledge.

Emit only the minimal answer string supported by the visible summaries in the `answer` field. Do not include explanations, justification, or any additional text.

Preserve multi-hop composition: you may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts across the summaries to form a licensed conclusion even when no single sentence states the whole relation. Distinguish licensed composition from unsupported inference; emit uncertainty only when a required premise, identity link, or relation is genuinely missing, ambiguous, or conflicting in the summaries.

Minimal-answer formatting rule (explicit):
- If the summaries jointly support one minimal literal and that literal appears verbatim in the summaries and consists of multiple contiguous tokens (for example: a full personal name, a full date, or a place name), emit that full literal exactly as it appears in the summaries (verbatim, unchanged). Do not truncate or coerce it to a single token.
- If the jointly supported minimal answer is itself a single-token label, emit exactly that single token (in lowercase) and nothing else.
- When the summaries contain both a short single-token alias and, in the later integrated summary (summary_2), a contiguous multi-token full-name literal that explicitly identifies or is explicitly linked to the same person (for example via an identity link or biographical framing in summary_2), prefer and emit that multi-token full-name literal exactly as it appears in summary_2. Do not prefer the longer form unless summary_2 provides an explicit identity link tying the alias to the full-name literal; if no such explicit link is present, follow the base identity-link rules and do not force selection of the longer form.
- Vocal-role single-token normalization (narrow scope): If summary_2 (alone or composed with summary_1 where identity links are explicit) explicitly contains vocal-role evidence — i.e., one or more verbalized vocal-role phrases that clearly denote a singing performer (examples: "lead singer", "lead vocals", "vocalist" as presented in the summaries) — then you may prefer to emit the concise single-token common noun for a person who sings (e.g., "singer") only when that single-token common noun itself appears verbatim in the summaries or when the vocal-role phrases in the summaries unambiguously denote the same person-role. If those explicit lexical licenses are not present, retain the literal role phrasing as given in summary_2 and do not normalize modifiers or drop qualifiers.

When in doubt about whether the summaries license a single definitive literal, prefer to emit uncertainty only if the required supporting premise or identity link is actually missing, ambiguous, or conflicting. Do not make uncertainty the default when composition or transitive reasoning is required and the necessary premises are present.

Ensure the `answer` field contains only the minimal answer string supported by the summaries (subject to the formatting rule above).

Due to poor sales for his previous album, "Out of Control", "Let Me Rock You" was not released in the United States until 1998, when it was reissued on CD.

response.hop1_query: In which year was the album released which included the single "Long Kiss Goodnight", which was used as a sample in the song "Rap name"?
hop1_docs: Rap Name | "Rap Name" is the debut single of American rapper Obie Trice, released as a limited edition vinyl recording, taken from the deluxe edition of the soundtrack to the film "8 Mile".  The song features vocals from fellow-label mate Eminem, who sampled the track in two tracks for his album "The Eminem Show", released earlier in 2002.  These tracks are "Drips" and the intro of the album's lead single, "Without Me", as well as a few other lines from the song.  The song also features a vocal sample of The Notorious B.I.G. from his song "Long Kiss Goodnight" from the album "Life After Death".  The official video was filmed in Detroit with featured cameo appearances by Eminem's group D12.

Church (song) | "Church" is a song by rapper T-Pain.  It is a fast-paced song led by guitar riffs and is one of the songs in which T-Pain raps.  It appears on his album "Epiphany" and was released as the third single.  The song "features" Teddy Verseti, one of T-Pain's aliases, which he uses when rapping vigorously.  The song is about wanting to fight someone following a confrontation at a club.  It was confirmed as the third single from "Epiphany" in an interview promoting the album.  It contains a sample of the song "Which Way to America" by Living Colour.

The Game (Alyssa Reid song) | "The Game" is a song by "The Next Star" contestant Alyssa Reid from her 2011 album "The Game".  It is the title track of the album and the album's second single, as well as Alyssa's second most famous single, beat out by her previous single "Alone Again", officially released May 31st, 2011.  It was preceded by the album's lead single, "Alone Again", featuring P. Reign, which heavily sampled the 1987 Heart song "Alone", except included new verses and rap.  "The Game" samples parts of the new verses of "Alone Again".

Goodnight Kisses | "Goodnight Kisses" is a song by American R&B singer Charlie Wilson.  It was released on October 7, 2014.  The song is the first single from the seventh studio album "Forever Charlie" (2015).

Sons of the P | Sons of the P is the second album by American rap group Digital Underground, released on October 15, 1991.  The album featured two hit singles, "No Nose Job" and "Kiss You Back", both of which were written by, and featured the lead vocals of Greg Jacobs (a.k.a. Shock G/Humpty Hump), and the latter of which featured multi-layered choruses and background vocals sung by Boni Boyer, who briefly worked with D.U. shortly after her stint with Prince's Sign of the Times/Love Sexy band.  It is sometimes mistakenly reported that Kiss You Back was co-written & co-performed by George Clinton, but his name appropriately appears in the writers credit due to a sample of "(Not Just) Knee Deep" by Funkadelic.  He did however actively participate in the writing and recording of the title track "Sons of the P", to which he also contributed vocals, and which marked one of the earliest studio guest appearances by Clinton on a Hip Hop release, preceded only by Kurtis Blow's "Magilla Gorilla" released in 1986.  Both the album and the single "Kiss You Back" were each certified Gold by the RIAA.

Fatherhood (album) | Fatherhood was the third of five albums of home-made recordings, released by Stephen Jones under the name Baby Bird in 1995.  The album includes a version of the song "Goodnight", which was subsequently released as the first single by Jones' band Babybird (written as one word).

Kiss You Back | "Kiss You Back" was the lead single released from Digital Underground's second studio album (their third official release), "Sons of the P".  The song sampled Funkadelic's 1979 hit, "(Not Just) Knee Deep" for which the song's writers, George Clinton and Philippé Wynne, received writing credits.
response.summary_1: Passage [1] states: "Rap Name ... features a vocal sample of The Notorious B.I.G. from his song 'Long Kiss Goodnight' from the album 'Life After Death'." Bridge entity: the album "Life After Death" (by The Notorious B.I.G.). Missing fact: the release year of the album "Life After Death." Retrieval direction: look up "Life After Death release year" or "Life After Death album release date The Notorious B.I.G." (verify the album's release year).
response.hop2_query: Life After Death The Notorious B.I.G. release year
hop2_docs: Life After Death | Life After Death is the second and final studio album by American rapper The Notorious B.I.G., released on March 25, 1997, on Bad Boy Records and Arista Records.  A double album, it was released posthumously following his death on March 9, 1997.  It features collaborations with guest artists such as 112, Jay-Z, Lil' Kim, Mase, Bone Thugs-n-Harmony, Too $hort, Angela Winbush, D.M.C. of Run-D.M.C. , R. Kelly, The LOX and Puff Daddy.  "Life After Death" exhibits The Notorious B.I.G. further delving into the mafioso rap subgenre.  The album is a sequel to his first album, "Ready to Die", and picks up where the last song, "Suicidal Thoughts", ends.

Hypnotize (The Notorious B.I.G. song) | "Hypnotize" is a single by American rapper The Notorious B.I.G., released as the first single from his album "Life After Death" on March 1, 1997 and the last song released in his lifetime, as he was killed in a drive-by shooting a week later.  It was the fifth song by a credited artist to hit number one posthumously.  It was ranked at number 30 on "Rolling Stone"' s 50 greatest rap songs of all time in hip hop history.

Sky's the Limit (The Notorious B.I.G. song) | "Sky's the Limit" is the third single from The Notorious B.I.G.'s second album "Life After Death".  It features vocals from R&B group 112 and somber production from Clark Kent.  It contains a sample from the songs "Is This Love" by Whitesnake, "My Flame" by Bobby Caldwell and "Keep On" by D. Train.  In the US, it was released as a triple A-side along with "Going Back to Cali" and "Kick In the Door".  The track was certified Gold by the RIAA in 1998.

Mo Money Mo Problems | "Mo Money Mo Problems" is a single by The Notorious B.I.G., the second single from his album "Life After Death".  Released posthumously, the single topped the "Billboard" Hot 100 for two weeks in 1997, replacing "I'll Be Missing You" from the chart, Puff Daddy's tribute to the rapper's death himself.  The song is Notorious B.I.G.'s second posthumous number one single, following "Hypnotize", making him the only artist in Hot 100 history to have two #1 singles posthumously.  It was the sixth song to hit #1 posthumously for a credited artist.  The song was nominated for the 1998 Grammy Award for Best Rap Performance by a Duo or Group.
Daniel Dancer | Daniel Dancer (1716–1794) was a notorious English miser whose life was documented soon after his death and continued in print over the following century.

Going Back to Cali (The Notorious B.I.G. song) | "Going Back to Cali" is a song recorded by rapper The Notorious B.I.G. and Puff Daddy (uncredited) from the album "Life After Death".  The song is noted as one of Biggie's popular songs from the album, well known for its catchy hook and beat.  The song contains a sample of Zapp hit "More Bounce to the Ounce".

Ready to Die | Ready to Die is the debut studio album by American rapper The Notorious B.I.G.; it was released on September 13, 1994, by Bad Boy Records and Arista Records.  The label's first release, the album features production by Bad Boy founder Sean "Puffy" Combs, Easy Mo Bee, Chucky Thompson, DJ Premier and Lord Finesse, among others.  Recording for the album took place during 1993-94 at The Hit Factory and D&D Studios in New York City.  The partly autobiographical album tells the story of B.I.G.'s experiences as a young criminal.  "Ready to Die" was the only studio album released during his life, as he was murdered sixteen days prior to the release of his second album, "Life After Death" in 1997.
